items.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
items.correct: They are carried in the bloodstream and act on cells with specific receptors for that hormone.
items.misconception: Hormones are sent through nerves directly to specific organs.
items.random_wrong: Hormones are released directly into target organs by being injected by glands.
items.why: Students conflate nervous and endocrine signalling; they think every signal in the body travels through nerves.

items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: Does an electrical impulse jump straight across a synapse?
items.correct: No — the impulse causes release of a neurotransmitter that diffuses across the gap to the next neurone.
items.misconception: The electrical signal leaps directly across the synapse.
items.random_wrong: The neurones touch and pass current directly.
items.why: Students picture a spark crossing, but transmission is chemical, which is why synapses make signals travel in only one direction.

items.correct: No — insulin lowers blood glucose; glucagon raises it.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin has no effect on blood glucose.
items.why: Students assume a glucose hormone increases glucose, but insulin reduces it by promoting uptake and storage as glycogen.

items.q: A reflex protects you because it is:
items.why: Correct answer: Fast and automatic. “Slow but accurate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Insulin. “Glucagon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 37. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
